items.evidence_text:
Client: Hi Alex. Honestly, I make good money but I feel behind—like the debt and the monthly burn are just… loud.
Advisor: I hear you. Let’s get a clean snapshot first, then we can talk priorities. Roughly what’s your total gross income for the year, all sources combined?
Client: Total gross is 265900 a year.
Advisor: Got it. And that’s coming from a few streams, right—business income, a salary, rental income, and then interest and dividends?
Client: Yes—business income, salary, rental income, and interest & dividends. The business one is kind of lumpy.

Client: Yes, annual. Interest & dividends is 121,300 annually.
Advisor: Perfect. One sec—just noting that down. Now on the spending side: when you look at everything going out each month, what’s your total monthly expenses number?
Client: Total monthly expenses are 21050.
Advisor: Okay. And inside that, the debt payments are the big pressure point. About how much are you paying per month across all debts combined?
Client: Monthly debt payments total is 15100.

Client: Total monthly expenses are 21050.
Advisor: Okay. And inside that, the debt payments are the big pressure point. About how much are you paying per month across all debts combined?
Client: Monthly debt payments total is 15100.
Advisor: Thanks. Let’s name the two debts so we can see what’s driving that. First, the mortgage—what’s the current balance and rate?
Client: Mortgage balance outstanding is 117,500 and the interest rate is 5.1%.

Client: Monthly payment is 800.
Advisor: Okay. Let’s switch to what you’ve built on the asset side. When you add up your investable accounts—brokerage, retirement, and the other invested buckets—what’s the total investable assets figure you think of?
Client: Investable assets total is 259350.
Advisor: And just to make sure I’m capturing the pieces: you’ve got a taxable brokerage account—what’s that sitting at?
Client: The taxable brokerage is 156,400.

Client: The bank-type one is 33,100, and the private markets one is 19,100.
Advisor: Got it. And you also have your home—what do you use as the current value for your primary residence?
Client: Primary residence value is 169,600.
Advisor: Thanks. Last category I want to make sure we cover today is protection—do you have any long-term care coverage in place?
Client: Yes, I have an LTC policy.

Client: I use 169600 for the property value.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And then on the debt side, if we add up everything you owe across loans, what do you have as total loans outstanding?
Client: Total loans outstanding is 140050.
Advisor: Okay. Just so I’m not double-counting anything—within that, the mortgage balance is what you said earlier, right?
Client: Yes—mortgage balance outstanding is 117500.

Client: Monthly debt payments total is 15100.
Advisor: Thanks. Let’s name the two debts so we can see what’s driving that. First, the mortgage—what’s the current balance and rate?
Client: Mortgage balance outstanding is 117,500 and the interest rate is 5.1%.
Advisor: And what’s the monthly payment on that mortgage?
Client: Monthly payment is 14,300.

Client: Final payment date is 2034-04-20.
Advisor: Great. Then the other one is the credit card—what’s the balance and interest rate there?
Client: Credit card balance outstanding is 22,550 and the interest rate is 8.92%.
Advisor: And the monthly payment you’re making on the card?
Client: Monthly payment is 800.

Advisor: Got it. And that’s coming from a few streams, right—business income, a salary, rental income, and then interest and dividends?
Client: Yes—business income, salary, rental income, and interest & dividends. The business one is kind of lumpy.

Advisor: Hi Mary—good to meet you. I’m Alex. Before we dig into numbers, what made you want to talk now?
Client: Hi Alex. Honestly, I make good money but I feel behind—like the debt and the monthly burn are just… loud.
Advisor: I hear you. Let’s get a clean snapshot first, then we can talk priorities. Roughly what’s your total gross income for the year, all sources combined?

Advisor: Hi Mary—good to meet you. I’m Alex. Before we dig into numbers, what made you want to talk now?
Client: Hi Alex. Honestly, I make good money but I feel behind—like the debt and the monthly burn are just… loud.
Advisor: I hear you. Let’s get a clean snapshot first, then we can talk priorities. Roughly what’s your total gross income for the year, all sources combined?
Client: Total gross is 265900 a year.
Advisor: Got it. And that’s coming from a few streams, right—business income, a salary, rental income, and then interest and dividends?

Advisor: And which state are you a resident of?
Client: NC.
Advisor: Thanks. And marital status—are you currently single, married, divorced, anything like that?
Client: I’m single.
Advisor: Perfect—thank you. One sec, I’m just noting those down.

Advisor: Okay. And that lines up with what you said earlier about not wanting to sell investments at a bad time.
Advisor: On taxes—ballpark, where do you usually land federally? Like 22, 24, 32, 35… that kind of bracket band.
Client: It’s 35%.
Advisor: Thanks. That helps me not recommend something that looks good pre-tax but stings after-tax.
Advisor: Last admin-ish thing: how are we working together—are you looking for an RIA relationship, like ongoing advice and management, versus just a one-time plan?

Advisor: Got it. And just so I label things correctly in my notes—are you okay being listed as Client 1?
Client: Yes, I’m Client 1.
Advisor: Perfect. Next—risk. When markets swing around, do you see yourself as more conservative, moderate, or aggressive?
Client: Aggressive. My risk tolerance is aggressive.
Advisor: Okay. And that lines up with what you said earlier about not wanting to sell investments at a bad time.

Client: It’s 35%.
Advisor: Thanks. That helps me not recommend something that looks good pre-tax but stings after-tax.
Advisor: Last admin-ish thing: how are we working together—are you looking for an RIA relationship, like ongoing advice and management, versus just a one-time plan?
Client: Yes—RIA (registered investment advisor).
Advisor: Great. And Mary, just to make sure I’m not missing anything big: we’ve got the four income streams, the five assets including the home, the two debts—mortgage and credit card—and the long-term care policy. That’s the full picture you want us planning around, right?

Client: It does. Rental income is 24,400 annually, paid monthly.
Advisor: And then interest and dividends—more of an annual total?
Client: Yes, annual. Interest & dividends is 121,300 annually.
Advisor: Perfect. One sec—just noting that down. Now on the spending side: when you look at everything going out each month, what’s your total monthly expenses number?
Client: Total monthly expenses are 21050.

Client: I’m employed—well, I run my business too, but yes, employed.
Advisor: Got it—employed. And in terms of the household, you’re the primary person making the financial calls, right?

Advisor: And you mentioned two other invested accounts that are held through an insurance company—one that’s more like a bank-style bucket and one that’s private markets. What are those values?
Client: The bank-type one is 33,100, and the private markets one is 19,100.

Client: Primary residence value is 169,600.
Advisor: Thanks. Last category I want to make sure we cover today is protection—do you have any long-term care coverage in place?

Client: Thank you. I’m fine with numbers, I just don’t want to spiral on them.
Advisor: Totally hear you. We’ll keep it tight and action-oriented.
Advisor: Okay—totally fair, Mary. We’ll keep it tight. One thing I want to capture cleanly because it affects taxes and planning assumptions: how would you describe what you do day to day—are you primarily an employee, a business owner, contractor…?
Client: I’m a business owner.
Advisor: Got it. And when you said earlier “employed,” is that like you’re on payroll through your own company, or you also have a separate W-2 job?

Client: Yes—business income, salary, rental income, and interest & dividends. The business one is kind of lumpy.
Advisor: Okay. If we break those out just so I’m not missing anything—what’s the business income number you use for the year?
Client: Business income is 78,200 annually, but it’s ad hoc—some months nothing, some months a lot.
Advisor: And your salary—paid weekly, you said?
Client: Yeah, weekly. Annual salary is 42,000.
